general_questions.text: What does it mean for a sequence to converge?
aliases: What does it mean for a sequence to converge to a limit L?
reference_answers: A sequence converges when its terms get arbitrarily close to a single limit after some index.

general_questions.text: Is every bounded sequence convergent? Why or why not?
reference_answers: No. A bounded sequence need not converge, because it can oscillate without approaching a single limit.

general_questions.text: Can you state the epsilon-delta definition of a limit or continuity?
reference_answers: For every epsilon greater than zero there exists a delta greater than zero such that the input being within delta forces the output within epsilon.

general_questions.text: Would you prefer to start with intuition, a worked example, or the formal definition?
aliases: Would you prefer intuition first or the formal definition first?
Would you prefer to start with the intuition or the formal proof?
reference_answers: I would like intuition first.
Please start with a worked example.
I prefer the formal definition first.

misconception_probe_questions.text: True or false: every bounded sequence converges.
reference_answers: False. Boundedness alone does not force convergence.

misconception_probe_questions.text: True or false: if a function is continuous on an open interval, it is uniformly continuous.
reference_answers: False. Continuity on an open interval does not always imply uniform continuity.

question_templates.text: What does it mean for a sequence to be bounded?
reference_answers: A sequence is bounded when all its terms stay within some fixed interval.

question_templates.text: What does it mean for a sequence to converge to a limit L?
aliases: What does it mean for a sequence to converge?
reference_answers: It means the terms eventually get arbitrarily close to L.

question_templates.text: Do you think a convergent sequence could be unbounded? Why or why not?
reference_answers: No. If a sequence converges, then eventually it stays near the limit, so it cannot be unbounded.

question_templates.text: Would you prefer to start with the intuition or the formal proof?
aliases: Would you prefer intuition first or the formal proof first?
reference_answers: Intuition first.
Formal proof first.

question_templates.text: What is the definition of continuity at a point?
reference_answers: A function is continuous at a point if inputs close to the point make outputs close to the function value.

question_templates.text: What is the difference between pointwise and uniform continuity?
reference_answers: Uniform continuity uses one delta that works across the whole domain, not a different delta at each point.

question_templates.text: Can you give an example of a continuous function that is not uniformly continuous?
reference_answers: A standard example is x squared on the whole real line.

question_templates.text: Would you like intuition first or the formal definition first?
aliases: Would you prefer intuition first or the formal definition first?
reference_answers: Intuition first.
Formal definition first.

question_templates.text: What is a Cauchy sequence?
reference_answers: A Cauchy sequence has terms that become arbitrarily close to each other after some point.

question_templates.text: Is every convergent sequence a Cauchy sequence?
reference_answers: Yes. In a metric space every convergent sequence is Cauchy.

question_templates.text: What is the difference between a Cauchy sequence and a convergent sequence?
reference_answers: Convergent sequences approach a limit, while Cauchy sequences only require the terms to get close to each other.

question_templates.text: Would you prefer to start with the definition or an example?
reference_answers: Definition first.
An example first.

question_templates.text: What does it mean for a sequence to be bounded?
reference_answers: It means all terms stay inside some fixed bound.

question_templates.text: What is a subsequence?
reference_answers: A subsequence is a new sequence formed by taking terms from the original sequence in increasing index order.

question_templates.text: Do you know any theorem about bounded sequences and convergence?
reference_answers: The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem says every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence.

question_templates.text: Would you prefer intuition first or the formal statement first?
reference_answers: Intuition first.
Formal statement first.

question_templates.text: What does it mean for a function to be continuous on an interval?
reference_answers: It means the function is continuous at every point of the interval.

question_templates.text: If f(a) < 0 and f(b) > 0, what would you expect about f somewhere between a and b?
reference_answers: I would expect the function to take the value zero somewhere between a and b.

question_templates.text: Can you think of a case where a continuous function skips a value?
reference_answers: No. A continuous function on an interval does not skip intermediate values.

question_templates.text: Would you like to start with the intuition or the formal proof?
reference_answers: Intuition first.
Formal proof first.
